texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Use of a Permeation Sampler for Determination of Volatile Priority Pollutants
texts.text: Use of a Permeation Sampler for Determination of Volatile Priority Pollutants

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Roger D. Blanchard and James K. Hardy*
texts.text: Roger D. Blanchard and James K. Hardy*

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Department of Chemistry, The University of Akron, Akron, Ohio 44326
texts.text: Department of Chemistry, The University of Akron, Akron, Ohio 44326

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A method for the sampling and subsequent analysis of six volatile organic Priority Pollutants Is described. Samples are collected by permetatlon through a silicone polycarbonate membrane and adsorbed onto activated charcoal. Analysis consists of sample desorption and quantitative measurement by gas chromatography. Linearity Is obtained between the mass of analyte collected and the product of the external concentration of analyte and the time of exposure, with linearity observed from low parts per billion to mid parts per million. This method has the advantages of providing timeweighted-average concentration values, stabilizing the collected sample, requiring no power, and being simple and Inexpensive to use. The device response time Is less than 5 min for each compound. No significant Interferences are observed but response Is temperature dependent. Precision and accuracy are comparable to presently accepted methods of collection and analysis.
texts.text: A method for the sampling and subsequent analysis of six volatile organic Priority Pollutants Is described. Samples are collected by permetatlon through a silicone polycarbonate membrane and adsorbed onto activated charcoal. Analysis consists of sample desorption and quantitative measurement by gas chromatography. Linearity Is obtained between the mass of analyte collected and the product of the external concentration of analyte and the time of exposure, with linearity observed from low parts per billion to mid parts per million. This method has the advantages of providing timeweighted-average concentration values, stabilizing the collected sample, requiring no power, and being simple and Inexpensive to use. The device response time Is less than 5 min for each compound. No significant Interferences are observed but response Is temperature dependent. Precision and accuracy are comparable to presently accepted methods of collection and analysis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Federal regulations which include the Federal Water Pollution Control Act of 1972 (2), the Clean Water Act of 1977 (2), and a lawsuit termed the Consent Decree (3) provided the impetus for the formation of the Priority Pollutant List (4). Contained in this list is a group of 31 species classified as volatile or purgeable organics.
texts.text: Federal regulations which include the Federal Water Pollution Control Act of 1972 (2), the Clean Water Act of 1977 (2), and a lawsuit termed the Consent Decree (3) provided the impetus for the formation of the Priority Pollutant List (4). Contained in this list is a group of 31 species classified as volatile or purgeable organics.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A number of procedures have been developed for collection and determination of volatile organics (5). The present federally approved method for their determination is the purge and trap method (6, 7) in which water is collected as a grab sample or by using an evacuated container or pump and then transported from the sampling site to a laboratory where refrigerated storage may be necessary. The analysis is conducted by first purging the volatile components from a measured volume of water with an inert gas, trapping on an appropriate medium such as Chromosorb 103 or Tenax GC, and then analyzing by thermal desorption and by GC or GC/MS.
texts.text: A number of procedures have been developed for collection and determination of volatile organics (5). The present federally approved method for their determination is the purge and trap method (6, 7) in which water is collected as a grab sample or by using an evacuated container or pump and then transported from the sampling site to a laboratory where refrigerated storage may be necessary. The analysis is conducted by first purging the volatile components from a measured volume of water with an inert gas, trapping on an appropriate medium such as Chromosorb 103 or Tenax GC, and then analyzing by thermal desorption and by GC or GC/MS.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These collection procedures have a number of inadequacies. Results obtained from grab sampling are limited in that the sample only represents the conditions existing at the single point in time when the sample was taken. As concentrations can be expected to vary with time, some form of continuous sampling is often more desirable. While sampling systems that involve a pump or evacuated container can be used on a continuous basis, they are typically of limited capacity and as a result can only be used for relatively short time intervals. Once the sample has been collected, some form of on-site refrigeration must often be employed to prevent loss of the volatile components. These systems are typically bulky, expensive, and may require an external power source, all of which act to make remote or multilocation sampling difficult if not prohibitive.
texts.text: These collection procedures have a number of inadequacies. Results obtained from grab sampling are limited in that the sample only represents the conditions existing at the single point in time when the sample was taken. As concentrations can be expected to vary with time, some form of continuous sampling is often more desirable. While sampling systems that involve a pump or evacuated container can be used on a continuous basis, they are typically of limited capacity and as a result can only be used for relatively short time intervals. Once the sample has been collected, some form of on-site refrigeration must often be employed to prevent loss of the volatile components. These systems are typically bulky, expensive, and may require an external power source, all of which act to make remote or multilocation sampling difficult if not prohibitive.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The following describes a method for the collection, concentration, and determination of six volatile Priority Pollutants which eliminates the inadequacies of present sampling procedures. The method is based on permeation methods developed for ambient air sampling (8-22). A membrane is used to affect collection of compounds from the surrounding environment and the species are then trapped on the opposite surface of the membrane. The device has the advantages of low cost, simplicity, the ability to stabilize the sample, and, due to its passive nature, no power requirements.
texts.text: The following describes a method for the collection, concentration, and determination of six volatile Priority Pollutants which eliminates the inadequacies of present sampling procedures. The method is based on permeation methods developed for ambient air sampling (8-22). A membrane is used to affect collection of compounds from the surrounding environment and the species are then trapped on the opposite surface of the membrane. The device has the advantages of low cost, simplicity, the ability to stabilize the sample, and, due to its passive nature, no power requirements.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The proposed method utilizes a silicone polycarbonate membrane and a collection medium of activated charcoal. After exposure of the sampling cell to the aqueous media of interest, the charcoal is removed, the volatile components are desorbed with carbon disulfide, a proven desorption solvent (22), and their levels are determined by gas chromatography.
texts.text: The proposed method utilizes a silicone polycarbonate membrane and a collection medium of activated charcoal. After exposure of the sampling cell to the aqueous media of interest, the charcoal is removed, the volatile components are desorbed with carbon disulfide, a proven desorption solvent (22), and their levels are determined by gas chromatography.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The species used for this study were benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, dichloromethane, chloroform, and carbon tetrachloride. These compounds were selected due to their relative frequency of occurrence in industrial wastewaters (23).
texts.text: The species used for this study were benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, dichloromethane, chloroform, and carbon tetrachloride. These compounds were selected due to their relative frequency of occurrence in industrial wastewaters (23).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: EXPERIMENTAL SECTION
texts.text: EXPERIMENTAL SECTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Apparatus. Initial membrane evaluation was conducted by use of the exposure cell shown in Figure 1. With this cell, one side of the membrane is exposed to an aqueous solution of a single test species. The other side has a nitrogen flow across it (10 mL/min) that is directed to a flame ionization detector (Varían Aerograph, 1200 series). The cell was designed such that the volume of either side was 1 mL and the exposed membrane area was 0.8 cm2. This cell was only used to evaluate the linearity and time of response of the membrane for single species.
texts.text: Apparatus. Initial membrane evaluation was conducted by use of the exposure cell shown in Figure 1. With this cell, one side of the membrane is exposed to an aqueous solution of a single test species. The other side has a nitrogen flow across it (10 mL/min) that is directed to a flame ionization detector (Varían Aerograph, 1200 series). The cell was designed such that the volume of either side was 1 mL and the exposed membrane area was 0.8 cm2. This cell was only used to evaluate the linearity and time of response of the membrane for single species.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The sampling devices consisted of 17 mm i.d. glass tubing, 50 mm in length to which a silicone polycarbonate membrane (0.025 mm thick, General Electric) was affixed with silicone rubber cement. The exposure chambers were 4-L Erlenmeyer flasks in which the devices would be suspended, and magnetic stir bars were used to assure adequate circulation of the test solutions. Aluminum foil was used to cover the rubber stopper used to seal the container to prevent sample losses. The sampling device and exposure chamber are shown in Figure 2.
texts.text: The sampling devices consisted of 17 mm i.d. glass tubing, 50 mm in length to which a silicone polycarbonate membrane (0.025 mm thick, General Electric) was affixed with silicone rubber cement. The exposure chambers were 4-L Erlenmeyer flasks in which the devices would be suspended, and magnetic stir bars were used to assure adequate circulation of the test solutions. Aluminum foil was used to cover the rubber stopper used to seal the container to prevent sample losses. The sampling device and exposure chamber are shown in Figure 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The gas chromatograph employed for all analyses beyond the initial membrane evaluation was a Hewlett-Packard Model 5730A dual column unit equipped with twin FID detectors. Six foot by Vs in. stainless steel columns packed with 1% SP-1000 on 60/80 Carbopack B (Supelco, Inc.) were used for all analyses. Temperature programming was employed with a sequence consisting of an initial temperature of 100 °C with a 4-min hold time, a temperature programming rate change of 8 °C/min, and a final temperature of 220 °C.
texts.text: The gas chromatograph employed for all analyses beyond the initial membrane evaluation was a Hewlett-Packard Model 5730A dual column unit equipped with twin FID detectors. Six foot by Vs in. stainless steel columns packed with 1% SP-1000 on 60/80 Carbopack B (Supelco, Inc.) were used for all analyses. Temperature programming was employed with a sequence consisting of an initial temperature of 100 °C with a 4-min hold time, a temperature programming rate change of 8 °C/min, and a final temperature of 220 °C.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Reagents. The sample species and carbon disulfide were all reagent grade (Fisher Scientific). Molecular sieves were used to reduce impurities in all organic liquids. The purity of each reagent was verified by gas chromatography where only CS2 demonstrated any significant levels of contamination. It was determined that benzene was present at low levels and, as a result, blank analyses were routinely conducted and the blank value for benzene substracted from sample results. All other chemicals used were reagent grade where possible. Deionized water that had been passed through two charcoal filters (Barnstead) was used throughout and was routinely assayed for organic contaminates. The adsorbing medium used was 200 mesh activated charcoal which had been initially treated by heating to 350 °C with a nitrogen gas purge. Stock solutions were prepared by saturation of water. These solutions were kept refrigerated until used. Test solutions were then prepared by dilution of the stock solutions and were verified as to their concentration by gas chromatography.
texts.text: Reagents. The sample species and carbon disulfide were all reagent grade (Fisher Scientific). Molecular sieves were used to reduce impurities in all organic liquids. The purity of each reagent was verified by gas chromatography where only CS2 demonstrated any significant levels of contamination. It was determined that benzene was present at low levels and, as a result, blank analyses were routinely conducted and the blank value for benzene substracted from sample results. All other chemicals used were reagent grade where possible. Deionized water that had been passed through two charcoal filters (Barnstead) was used throughout and was routinely assayed for organic contaminates. The adsorbing medium used was 200 mesh activated charcoal which had been initially treated by heating to 350 °C with a nitrogen gas purge. Stock solutions were prepared by saturation of water. These solutions were kept refrigerated until used. Test solutions were then prepared by dilution of the stock solutions and were verified as to their concentration by gas chromatography.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
texts.text: RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Initial Membrane Evaluation. Linearity of response and response time of the membrane were initially evaluated by use of the permeation cell. It was observed that the rate of permeation through the membrane was a function of exposure solution concentration over a range of 1-50 ppm for each species. The response time was considered to be the time required to reach 90% response at a given concentration and was found to be <5 min in all cases. Representative results for benzene are given in Figure 3. It was noted that if the flow of the test solution was less than 0.5 mL/min through the cell, there was a subsequent decrease in the permeation rate, but at flows above this value, the rate remained constant. The process of mass transport across the membrane is a permeation controlled process not diffusion controlled. However, at low flow rates, the fact that sample species are being removed from the system can result in a concentration gradient being established resulting in the sampling becoming diffusion dependent. Sample mixing and turbulence are not a problem as long as the flow of sample is such that the solution in contact with the membrane remains representative of the bulk solution. Only when sampling from a static system
texts.text: Initial Membrane Evaluation. Linearity of response and response time of the membrane were initially evaluated by use of the permeation cell. It was observed that the rate of permeation through the membrane was a function of exposure solution concentration over a range of 1-50 ppm for each species. The response time was considered to be the time required to reach 90% response at a given concentration and was found to be <5 min in all cases. Representative results for benzene are given in Figure 3. It was noted that if the flow of the test solution was less than 0.5 mL/min through the cell, there was a subsequent decrease in the permeation rate, but at flows above this value, the rate remained constant. The process of mass transport across the membrane is a permeation controlled process not diffusion controlled. However, at low flow rates, the fact that sample species are being removed from the system can result in a concentration gradient being established resulting in the sampling becoming diffusion dependent. Sample mixing and turbulence are not a problem as long as the flow of sample is such that the solution in contact with the membrane remains representative of the bulk solution. Only when sampling from a static system

texts.label: text
texts.orig: should the depletion of sample species result in a low response. This problem can be reduced by producing a flow near the membrane to assure adequate mixing.
texts.text: should the depletion of sample species result in a low response. This problem can be reduced by producing a flow near the membrane to assure adequate mixing.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Calibration of Permeation Devices. Previous workers have shown that for ambient air permeation monitors, a linear relationship exists between the mass of analyte collected or a parameter related to mass and the product of its external concentration and the duration of exposure to the sampling device (8-11). To determine if such a relationship could be observed for the collection of the volatile Priority Pollutants being evaluated, devices were exposed to known levels of each for time periods ranging from 1 h to 10 days. In each case, three devices were exposed to identical solutions in three separate exposure chambers. Each device was charged with 1 g of activated charcoal just prior to exposure. After an appropriate exposure interval, the devices were withdrawn and the charcoal was removed. The samples were stored in 9-mL glass vials until analysis could be performed. For analysis, the charcoal was desorbed with 5 mL of carbon disulfide, the solution was allowed to stand for 30 min, and then 5 mL of this solution was assayed. It was found that a linear relationship was obtained between the mass of analyte collected and the product of its external concentration and time of exposure. The calibration constant (K) of each species for each device was determined by exposing it to a known concentration of each of the compounds. This constant is defined as
texts.text: Calibration of Permeation Devices. Previous workers have shown that for ambient air permeation monitors, a linear relationship exists between the mass of analyte collected or a parameter related to mass and the product of its external concentration and the duration of exposure to the sampling device (8-11). To determine if such a relationship could be observed for the collection of the volatile Priority Pollutants being evaluated, devices were exposed to known levels of each for time periods ranging from 1 h to 10 days. In each case, three devices were exposed to identical solutions in three separate exposure chambers. Each device was charged with 1 g of activated charcoal just prior to exposure. After an appropriate exposure interval, the devices were withdrawn and the charcoal was removed. The samples were stored in 9-mL glass vials until analysis could be performed. For analysis, the charcoal was desorbed with 5 mL of carbon disulfide, the solution was allowed to stand for 30 min, and then 5 mL of this solution was assayed. It was found that a linear relationship was obtained between the mass of analyte collected and the product of its external concentration and time of exposure. The calibration constant (K) of each species for each device was determined by exposing it to a known concentration of each of the compounds. This constant is defined as

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where C represents the time-weighted-average external concentration in parts per million, t is the exposure period in hours, m is mass collected (micrograms), and K, the calibration constant for each species, has units of Mg/(ppm-h). A linear relationship to cumulative exposure was verified by determining the mass of each species collected after exposure to various time intervals and concentrations. Figure 4 depicts a typical chromatograph obtained for the analysis. Table I lists the K values, ppm-h values, concentration ranges, and correlation coefficients for each compound at 20 °C.

A linear relationship to cumulative exposure was verified by determining the mass of each species collected after exposure to various time intervals and concentrations. Figure 4 depicts a typical chromatograph obtained for the analysis. Table I lists the K values, ppm-h values, concentration ranges, and correlation coefficients for each compound at 20 °C.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Statistical Evaluation of Devices. To determine the precision of the permeation method, replicate samples and injections of a single sample were analyzed. As can be seen in Table III, a variation of 12.00% was observed with multiple sampling for ethylbenzene (worse case), where a variation of 1.70% was observed for the analysis alone. The reported variation observed with the purge and trap method for sample recovery is generally ±20% (13). This indicates that the permeation technique provides results with a precision as good as the purge and trap method.
texts.text: Statistical Evaluation of Devices. To determine the precision of the permeation method, replicate samples and injections of a single sample were analyzed. As can be seen in Table III, a variation of 12.00% was observed with multiple sampling for ethylbenzene (worse case), where a variation of 1.70% was observed for the analysis alone. The reported variation observed with the purge and trap method for sample recovery is generally ±20% (13). This indicates that the permeation technique provides results with a precision as good as the purge and trap method.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sample Stability. Charcoal was exposed to a mixture of the six species being studied and then stored in a sealed container at room temperature. At regular intervals, a known amount of charcoal was removed and desorbed and the quantity of each species present determined. Over a 36-day périod, no significant loss of analyte was observed. Likewise, charcoal samples produced by exposure within the devices demonstrated no significant losses when stored for the same time period.
texts.text: Sample Stability. Charcoal was exposed to a mixture of the six species being studied and then stored in a sealed container at room temperature. At regular intervals, a known amount of charcoal was removed and desorbed and the quantity of each species present determined. Over a 36-day périod, no significant loss of analyte was observed. Likewise, charcoal samples produced by exposure within the devices demonstrated no significant losses when stored for the same time period.

texts.orig: what effect temperature changes would have. For this evaluation, exposure chambers were thermostated at seven temperatures over a range of 0.4-29.2 °C. Table IV lists results for changes in the permeation constant vs. temperature for each component. Increases in the permeation constant with temperature were observed for each compound investigated and approximates a linear relationship. Variations in temperature can be accounted for by using a modified form of eq 2 where the average temperature during exposure is known

Increases in the permeation constant with temperature were observed for each compound investigated and approximates a linear relationship. Variations in temperature can be accounted for by using a modified form of eq 2 where the average temperature during exposure is known

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: C = m/(K0 + sT)t (3)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where K0 is the permeation constant at 0 °C, T is the average temperature during exposure (°C), s is the slope of the temperature effect (  /  ), and C, m, and t have been previously defined. Time averaging of the temperature is necessary to obtain a representative permeation constant, though fluctuations in ambient water temperatures are typically minimal.
texts.text: where K0 is the permeation constant at 0 °C, T is the average temperature during exposure (°C), s is the slope of the temperature effect (  /  ), and C, m, and t have been previously defined. Time averaging of the temperature is necessary to obtain a representative permeation constant, though fluctuations in ambient water temperatures are typically minimal.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Interferences. The permeation method could be interfered with by a number of processes caused by chemical species or various conditions of the aqueous media. These processes could include factors which alter the permeation characteristics of the membrane or species that can alter the form of the compounds of interest before or after collection. The chemicals and conditions studied were chosen to assess the effect of strong oxidizers, volatile and nonvolatile acids, ionic strength variations, and surface active agents on the permeation process. Chemicals used in these studies include sodium chloride (8.6 X 1CT3 M), humic acid (2500 ppm), sodium hypochlorite (2.0 X 10'3 M), hydrochloric acid (0.15 M), sodium lauryl sulfate (3.0 X 10~3 M) and enhanced levels of dichloromethane (6600 ppm). The effect of pH was also evaluated over a range of 3.5-8.0. In each case, no significant variation in response was observed.
texts.text: Interferences. The permeation method could be interfered with by a number of processes caused by chemical species or various conditions of the aqueous media. These processes could include factors which alter the permeation characteristics of the membrane or species that can alter the form of the compounds of interest before or after collection. The chemicals and conditions studied were chosen to assess the effect of strong oxidizers, volatile and nonvolatile acids, ionic strength variations, and surface active agents on the permeation process. Chemicals used in these studies include sodium chloride (8.6 X 1CT3 M), humic acid (2500 ppm), sodium hypochlorite (2.0 X 10'3 M), hydrochloric acid (0.15 M), sodium lauryl sulfate (3.0 X 10~3 M) and enhanced levels of dichloromethane (6600 ppm). The effect of pH was also evaluated over a range of 3.5-8.0. In each case, no significant variation in response was observed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Spiked Authentic Samples. To assess the performance of the permeation devices in matrices representative of those encountered in the field, wastewater samples were obtained from a local treatment facility employing the activated sludge process. The water samples acquired represented three stages of the treatment: untreated, primary filtered, and secondary treated water. These samples were spiked with the six volatile organic compounds and their levels verified by GC. With each sample, a total of six exposures were conducted with each matrix to determine the effect that they would have. The results of these studies as well as several parameters used to assay water quality are given in Table V. The results indicate that the matrix has little effect on the sampler.
texts.text: Spiked Authentic Samples. To assess the performance of the permeation devices in matrices representative of those encountered in the field, wastewater samples were obtained from a local treatment facility employing the activated sludge process. The water samples acquired represented three stages of the treatment: untreated, primary filtered, and secondary treated water. These samples were spiked with the six volatile organic compounds and their levels verified by GC. With each sample, a total of six exposures were conducted with each matrix to determine the effect that they would have. The results of these studies as well as several parameters used to assay water quality are given in Table V. The results indicate that the matrix has little effect on the sampler.
